pattern: Because a jump can land on any cell in the same row or column (not just an adjacent one), the longest chain only cares about relative value order, so processing cells value-tier by value-tier and remembering the best chain seen so far per row and per column beats exploring every pair directly.
hints: A jump can land anywhere in the same row or column as long as the value strictly increases, so distance between cells never matters -- only which row/column a cell belongs to and its value.
Sort all cells by value and sweep them from smallest to largest, keeping a running best chain-length for every row and every column seen so far.

When several cells share the exact same value, compute all of their chain lengths using the row/column bests from strictly smaller values first, and only fold their own results into the row/column bests afterward -- equal-value cells must never chain off each other.

edge_inputs.description: small 2x2 with a repeated value blocking one path
every cell identical, no strictly greater neighbor exists anywhere
2x3 with a negative value and a mixed chain
single row, strictly increasing left to right
single column with values in decreasing physical order but still chainable by value
an entire row of repeated values, no increase possible within it
negative and zero values mixed across a small grid
4x4 grid with scattered values including negatives and repeats

stress.description: small random matrices with repeated values to stress the equal-value batching
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    dim = max(1, min(size, 8))
    m = rng.randint(1, dim)
    n = rng.randint(1, dim)
    mat = [[rng.randint(-50, 50) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [mat]
